1.translation: Now, everyone, do you know what this hazy white thing really is, which some say is a river and others say is the trail of spilled milk?" The teacher asked everyone, pointing from top to bottom to the whitish, cloud-like band of the Milky Way on a large constellation diagram hanging on the blackboard. Campanella raised his hand. Then four or five others raised their hands. Giovanni also started to raise his hand but quickly stopped. He had indeed read somewhere in a magazine that all those stars were stars, but recently Giovanni was so sleepy in class every day that he had no time to read books and no books to read, so he felt like he didn't understand anything anymore. But the teacher had already noticed him. "Giovanni, you understand, don't you?

2.translation: Giovanni stood up energetically, but when he stood up, he couldn't clearly answer the question. Zaneri turned back from the seat in front and looked at Giovanni, giving a slight laugh. Giovanni became flustered and turned bright red. The teacher said again, 'When you carefully observe the galaxy with a large telescope, what is it mostly made of?' Giovanni thought it was stars again, but this time too, he couldn't answer immediately. The teacher seemed troubled for a while, then turned his eyes toward Campanella and called his name, 'Then, Campanella-san.' But even Campanella, who had raised his hand so energetically, stood up fidgeting and still couldn't answer. The teacher looked at Campanella intently for a while, surprised, then quickly said, 'Then.'

3.translation: "Alright," he said, pointing to the star chart himself. "When you look at this faint white galaxy with a good telescope, you can see it's made up of many small stars, right Giovanni?" Giovanni nodded, his face bright red. But at some point, tears filled Giovanni's eyes. That's right, I knew it, of course Campanella knew it too - it was in that magazine that Campanella and I had read together at his father the professor's house. And not only that, whenever Campanella read that magazine, he would immediately bring a large book from his father's study, open the page labeled 'galaxy,' and the two of them would look at the beautiful photograph with white dots scattered across a completely black page for what seemed like forever. And yet Campanella couldn't possibly have forgotten that, so why didn't he answer right away? It must be because lately, I've been finding work difficult both morning and afternoon, and at school I don't play cheerfully with everyone anymore, and I hardly talk to Campanella either. So Campanella must have known this and felt sorry for me and deliberately didn't answer. Thinking about this, I couldn't help but feel that both Campanella and I were somehow pitiful.

4.translation: The teacher said again, 'Therefore, if you consider this Milky Way to be truly a river, then each of the small stars would all be like grains of sand or pebbles in that river. Furthermore, if you think of it as a great flow of milk, it becomes even more similar to the Milky Way. In other words, those stars are all like tiny globules of fat floating in the milk. Then you might ask what corresponds to the water of that river? It is the vacuum that transmits light at a certain speed, and the sun and earth are also floating within it. In other words, we too are dwelling within the water of the Milky Way. And when you look out in all directions from within this water of the Milky Way, just as water appears bluer the deeper it is, the stars appear more numerous and gather together, looking white and indistinct in the deeper, farther parts of the Milky Way.'

5.translation: This model, please look at it." The teacher pointed to a large double-convex lens filled with many shining grains of sand inside. "The shape of the Milky Way is just like this. We believe that each one of these shining grains is a star that shines on its own, just like our sun. Let's suppose our sun is located at approximately the center of this, and Earth is very close to it. Everyone, please imagine standing in the middle of this at night and looking around inside this lens. On this side, the lens is thin, so only a few shining particles, that is, stars, would be visible. On these and these other sides, the glass is thick, so many shining particles, that is, stars, are visible, and the distant ones appear vaguely white. This is, in fact, today's theory of the galaxy. Well, as for the size of this lens and the various stars within it, we've run out of time, so I'll talk about it in the next science class.

6.translation: Now today is that festival of the galaxy, so everyone, please go outside and look carefully at the sky. Well, that's all for today. Please put away your books and notebooks." Then for a while, the classroom was filled with the sounds of opening and closing desk lids and stacking books, but soon everyone stood up properly, bowed, and left the classroom. When Giovanni exited the school gate, seven or eight students from his class didn't go home but gathered around Campanella in the corner of the school yard under a cherry tree. It seemed they were discussing going to get the eggplant-like gourds with blue lights to float down the river for tonight's Star Festival. But Giovanni waved his hand broadly and came striding out of the school gate. Then in the town houses, people were preparing for tonight's Galaxy Festival by hanging balls of iris leaves on branches and attaching lights to cypress branches, making various preparations.

7.translation: Instead of going home, Giovanni turned three corners in town and entered a large printing office. He bowed to a man in a loose white shirt at the calculation desk by the entrance, removed his shoes, and went upstairs. He then opened a large door at the end. Inside, even though it was daytime, electric lights were on, and many rotary machines were spinning with a clattering sound. People were working hard while singing-like reading or counting, cutting type with knives, and putting lampshades on them. Giovanni went to the third tall desk from the entrance and bowed to the person sitting there. After searching through some shelves for a while, that person said, 'Can you carry all these?' while handing him a piece of paper. Giovanni took a small flat box from under the desk, crouched in a corner of the wall where many electric lights were mounted, and began to pick up type characters as small as millet grains with small tweezers.

8.translation: The person with the blue breastplate, passing behind Giovanni, said, 'Well, Mr. Magnifying Glass, good morning.' Then, four or five people nearby laughed coldly without making a sound or looking this way. Giovanni wiped his eyes several times as he gradually spread out the type. It was a little after six o'clock when Giovanni, comparing the flat box he was holding, which was full of the type he had picked up, with a piece of paper, brought it to the person at the desk earlier. That person silently received it and gave a slight nod. Giovanni bowed, opened the door, and came back to the calculating desk from earlier. Then, the person in the white clothes from earlier was still silently handing a small silver coin to Giovanni. Giovanni's face suddenly brightened, and he bowed energetically, then grabbed his bag from under the desk and dashed outside.

9.translation: After that, whistling cheerfully, he stopped by the bakery to buy a loaf of bread and a bag of sugar cubes, then immediately ran off. Giovanni returned energetically to a small house in a back alley. On the left side of the three entrances, there were kale and asparagus planted in empty boxes, and the two small windows still had their blinds down. 'Mother, I'm home now. Was everything okay?' Giovanni said as he took off his shoes. 'Ah, Giovanni, your work must have been terrible. It's cool today. I've been feeling much better.' Giovanni went up to the entrance and saw his mother lying in the small room at the entrance, covered with a white cloth. Giovanni opened the window. 'Mother, I bought sugar cubes today. I thought I'd put them in your milk.'

10.translation: 「Ah, you go ahead first. I don't want any yet.」「Mother. When did sister come home?」「Oh, around three o'clock. Everyone helped out.」「Has the milk for Mother come yet?」「I suppose not.」「I'll go get it.」「Oh, take your time, I'm in no hurry. Sister made something with tomatoes and left it there for you.」「Then I'll eat.」Giovanni took the plate of tomatoes from the window and ate them with bread for a while.「Hey Mother. I'm sure Father will be home soon.」「Oh, I think so too. But why do you think that?」「Because this morning's paper said that the fishing in the north was very good this year.」

11.translation: 'But you know, Dad might not have gone fishing today.' 'He definitely has gone. Dad wouldn't have done anything bad enough to land him in prison. The big crab shell and the squid horn he donated to the school not long ago are still in the specimen room. The sixth-grade teachers take turns bringing them to class during lessons. The year before last on the school trip... 'Dad said he would bring you a sea otter jacket next time.' 'Everyone says that to me when they see me, teasing me.' 'Are they saying mean things to you?' 'Yeah, but Campanella never says such things. When everyone says those things, Campanella looks at me sympathetically.' 'I heard that his father was friends with my father since they were little, just like you two.'

12.translation: 「Yes, that's why my father took me to Campanella's house. Those were good times. I often stopped by Campanella's house on my way home from school. There was a train running on alcohol lamps in Campanella's house. When seven rails were combined, they formed a circle, and telephone poles and signal signs were attached to it. The light on the signal sign would only turn green when the train passed. When the alcohol ran out once and we used oil instead, the can got completely blackened.」"「Is that so?」"「I still go to deliver newspapers every morning. But the house is always quiet in the morning.」"「It's early, isn't it?」"「There's a dog named Sauer. Its tail is like a broom. When I go, it barks its nose and follows me. All the way to the corner of town.」

13.translation: You might want to come along too. They say tonight everyone's going to release the light of gourds down to the river. The dog will surely come along too. That's right. Tonight is the festival of the Milky Way. Yeah. I'll go get some milk and watch. Oh, go ahead. But don't go into the river. Oh, I'll just watch from the shore. I'll be back in an hour. Stay and play a bit longer. If you're with Campanella, there's nothing to worry about. Oh, I'll surely be with him. Mother, shall I close the window? Oh, please do. It's getting cool now. Giovanni stood up, closed the window, cleared the dishes and bread bags, quickly put on his boots, and saying I'll be back in an hour and a half, went out the dark doorway. Giovanni came down the town's slope lined with pitch-black cypresses, with a sad expression as if he were whistling.

vocabulary.translation: to tidy up, to clear away

14.translation: The large street lamp at the bottom of the hill was shining splendidly with a pale blue light. As Giovanni went down toward the electric lamp, his shadow, which had been following him like a ghost, long and indistinct, gradually became darker and clearer, moving to Giovanni's side as he lifted his feet or waved his hands. (I am a splendid locomotive. This is a slope, so I'm fast. I am now passing that electric lamp. Yes, this time my shadow is a compass. It turned around so quickly and came to the front.) As Giovanni thought this and passed quickly under the street lamp, suddenly Zaneri, wearing a new shirt with a pointed collar, came out from a dark alley on the other side of the lamp and brushed past Giovanni. 'Zaneri, are you going to wash the gourds?'

15.dayTitle: Giovanni's Journey Through the Ornate Street
15.translation: Before Giovanni could say anything, 'Giovanni, your father sent your coat,' the child called out from behind as if throwing something. Giovanni's chest suddenly felt cold, and he felt as if everything around him was ringing. 'What is that, Zanelli?' Giovanni shouted back, but Zanelli had already entered the house with birch trees planted on the other side. 'Why does Zanelli say such things when I haven't done anything? He runs like a mouse, yet he says such things when I haven't done anything. Zanelli must be stupid.' Giovanni thought about various things in a hurry as he passed through the beautifully decorated street with various lights and tree branches. In the clock shop, bright neon lights were on, and every second, the red eyes of an owl carved from stone would turn around and around, various gems were placed on a thick glass plate of sea-like color and slowly circled like stars, and from the other side, a copper centaur slowly turned toward him.

16.translation: Right in the center, there was a circular black constellation finder decorated with blue asparagus leaves. Giovanni forgot himself and stared intently at the constellation chart. It was much smaller than the one he had seen in daytime school, but when he turned the dial to match the day and time, the stars that were visible at that moment would appear to move within the elliptical frame. And right in the center, from top to bottom, there was a hazy, smoky band like the Milky Way, and below it, something seemed to be faintly exploding, like steam rising. Behind it, there stood a small telescope with three legs, shining yellow. And on the far wall, there was a large diagram depicting all the constellations in the sky in strange shapes like beasts, snakes, fish, and bottles. Could there really be so many scorpions and warriors crowded in the sky? Ah, I thought I'd like to walk among them forever. He stood there blankly for a while.

17.translation: After suddenly remembering his mother's milk, Giovanni left the shop. And while being concerned about the tight shoulders of his jacket, he nevertheless deliberately stood tall, waved his hand broadly, and walked through town. The air was crystal clear, flowing like water through the streets and shops. All the streetlights were wrapped in deep blue maple and oak branches, and the six plane trees in front of the electric company had many small electric lights inside them, making that area truly look like a city of mermaids. The children were all wearing newly pleated kimonos, whistling as they circled the stars, shouting 'Centaur, scatter the dew!' as they ran, and burning blue magnesium fireworks, playing happily. But Giovanni, at some point, once again lowered his head deeply, thinking something completely different from the bustle around him, as he hurried toward the dairy shop.

18.translation: Giovanni had come to a place where many poplar trees stood at the edge of town, towering high against the starry sky. He entered the black gate of the dairy, stood before the dimly lit kitchen that smelled of cows, and took off his hat. 'Good evening,' he said, but the house was so quiet it seemed no one was there. 'Good evening, sorry to bother you,' Giovanni said, standing straight and calling out again. After a while, an old woman came out slowly, as if something were wrong, and mumbled something in her mouth about what he wanted. 'Um, today, the milk didn't come to my place, so I came to get it,' Giovanni said with all his might. 'I don't know where anyone is right now. Please come back tomorrow.' The woman said, looking down at Giovanni while rubbing under her red-rimmed eyes.

vocabulary.translation: with all one's might/heart and soul
to look down on (literally or figuratively)

19.translation: 「My mother is sick, so I really need to go tonight.」「Then please come back a little later.」That person was about to leave.「Is that so. Then thank you.」Giovanni bowed and left the kitchen. When he tried to turn at the corner of the cross-shaped town, in front of the general store on the way to the opposite bridge, he saw six or seven students, with black shadows and blurry white shirts mixed together, coming while carrying lanterns made from dried gourds, whistling and laughing. That laughter and whistling were all things he remembered hearing. They were Giovanni's classmates. Giovanni was about to turn back in surprise, but then changed his mind and walked more briskly in that direction. 'Going to the river,' Giovanni was about to say when he felt his throat tighten a little.

20.translation: Joan, the raccoon coat is coming." Zaneri from earlier shouted again. "Joan, the raccoon coat is coming." Immediately everyone followed shouting. Joan turned bright red, and wasn't even sure if he was walking anymore, and tried to hurry past, but Campanella was there in the middle. Campanella looked at Joan pitifully, smiling a little, as if wondering if Joan would be angry. Joan avoided his eyes as if fleeing, and soon after Campanella's tall figure passed by, everyone whistled on their own. When turning a street corner, he looked back and saw that Zaneri was also looking back. And Campanella too, whistling loudly, walked toward the faintly visible bridge in the distance.

21.translation: Giovanni, feeling an indescribable sadness, suddenly started running. The small children, who had been hopping on one foot while covering their ears and shouting 'waah,' thought Giovanni was doing it for fun and shouted along with him. Soon, Giovanni hurried toward the black hill. Behind the pasture, the land formed a gentle slope, and the flat black summit of the hill appeared dimly and lower than usual beneath the Great Bear constellation in the north. Giovanni quickly climbed the small path in the forest where dew had fallen. Through the dark grass and the dense undergrowth of various shapes, the small path was illuminated by starlight, appearing as a single white streak. In the grass, there were small insects emitting a blue glow, some leaves were illuminated from behind, and Giovanni thought it resembled the light from the gourds that everyone had taken earlier.

vocabulary.translation: to show through, to be translucent

22.translation: As they crossed the pitch-black pine and oak forest, suddenly the sky opened up wide, and the Milky Way could be seen stretching brilliantly from south to north. Also, the weather wheel pillar on the summit became visible. Flowers like bellflowers or wild chrysanthemums bloomed all over the place as if emitting fragrance from a dream, and a bird passed by while continuously crying on the hilltop. Giovanni came under the weather wheel pillar on the summit and threw his tired body onto the cool grass. The town lights flickered in the darkness like the appearance of a shrine at the bottom of the sea, and the faint sounds of children singing, whistling, and sharp cries could be heard. The wind rang in the distance, the grass on the hills swayed gently, and Giovanni's sweat-soaked shirt was cooled by the cold air. Giovanni gazed at the distant, dark, and expansive fields from the edge of town.

23.translation: There came the sound of a train from there. The small windows of that little train could be seen in a row, small and red, and inside, many travelers were peeling apples, laughing, and doing various things, and when Giovanni thought of this, he became indescribably sad and again raised his eyes elsewhere. Ah, that white belt of sky is all stars, he thought. But no matter how much he looked, that sky did not seem to be the empty, cold place that Mr. Hiru had spoken of. On the contrary, the more he looked, the more it inevitably seemed to him like a small forest, pasture, or some field. And Giovanni saw that the blue lyre star became three, then four, twinkling, and its legs appeared and disappeared many times, and finally extended like a mushroom. Also, the town right below his eyes seemed to him to be just like a vague collection of many stars or like a single large smoke.

24.translation: And then Giovanni saw that the column of the weather wheel behind him gradually took the shape of a vague triangular marker, flickering like fireflies for a while. It gradually became clearer and finally stood straight in a field of deep blue steel-colored sky. It stood straight and tall in that sky field, newly scorched like a blue steel plate. Then, from somewhere, a mysterious voice called out, 'Galaxy Station, Galaxy Station.' Just as he thought he heard this voice, suddenly everything before his eyes became brilliantly bright. It was as if someone had instantly fossilized countless firefly squid and sunk them into the sky, or as if someone had suddenly overturned the diamonds that a diamond company had deliberately left unharvested to prevent their value from decreasing, scattering them about. Before his eyes, everything suddenly brightened, and Giovanni couldn't help but rub his eyes several times.

25.translation: When he came to his senses, he realized that for some time now, his small train had been running continuously. Giovanni was truly sitting in the small compartment of the night light railway, lined with small yellow electric lamps, looking out the window. Inside the compartment, the blue velvet-upholstered seats seemed completely empty, and on the opposite wall, painted with brown varnish, two large brass buttons were shining. He noticed that in the seat right in front, a tall child in a black, wet-looking coat was leaning out the window looking outside. And then, the area around the child's shoulder seemed somehow familiar to him. As soon as he thought this, he became terribly eager to know who it was. Just as he was about to suddenly lean out the window himself, the child suddenly pulled their head back and looked at him.

vocabulary.translation: when he came to his senses/realized
onomatopoeia for the sound of the train running

vocabulary.word: ぬれたようにまっ黒な

26.translation: It was Campanella. When Giovanni was about to say to Campanella, 'You've been here from the beginning, haven't you?', Campanella said, 'Everyone ran quite a bit, but they were late. Zanelli also ran quite a bit, but couldn't catch up.' As Giovanni thought, 'That's right, we were invited to go together,' he said, 'Shall we wait somewhere?' Then Campanella said, 'Zanelli has already returned. His father came to pick him up.' For some reason, as Campanella said this, his face turned pale, and he seemed to be in some pain. Then Giovanni also felt a strange feeling as if he had forgotten something somewhere, and fell silent. However, Campanella, looking out the window, had completely recovered and said energetically.

vocabulary.translation: was late, ended up being late
as if in pain, seeming to be suffering

27.translation: Oh no, I've forgotten my water bottle. I've forgotten my sketchbook too. But it doesn't matter. We'll be at the Swan Station soon. I really love to see swans. Even if they're flying far away on the river, I'm sure I'll be able to see them." And then, Campanella kept turning round and round a circular map-like plate. On it, along the left bank of the Milky Way shown in white, a railway line traced south and south. And what was magnificent about this map was that on a pitch-black board like night, various stations, triangular markers, springs, and forests were scattered with beautiful lights of blue, orange, green, and other colors. Giovanni felt as if he had seen this map somewhere. "Where did you buy this map? It looks like it's made of obsidian," Giovanni said.

28.translation: "At the Galactic Station, I got it. Didn't you get one?" "Ah, did I pass through the Galactic Station? I think this is where we are now," Giovanni said, pointing just north of the station sign that had "swan" written on it. "That's right. Oh, is that riverbank in the moonlight?" Looking that way, on the palely glowing bank of the galaxy, silver-colored sky pampas grass was already spread out like a field, swaying and rustling in the wind, creating waves. "It's not moonlight. It's glowing because it's the galaxy," Giovanni said, and becoming so happy he felt like jumping, he tapped his feet rhythmically, stuck his face out the window, whistling a star-seeking tune loudly while straining to stretch up as far as he could, trying to examine the water of the Milky Way, but at first he just couldn't see it clearly.

29.translation: But as he looked more carefully, that beautiful water was clearer than glass or hydrogen, and as it flowed silently and swiftly along, it occasionally created faint purple waves depending on the light, or sparkled like a rainbow. In the fields, here and there, beautiful phosphorescent triangular markers stood tall. The distant ones were small and the nearby ones large; the distant ones were clearly orange or yellow, while the nearby ones were pale blue and somewhat indistinct. They were arranged in various shapes - triangles, quadrilaterals, or shapes like lightning or chains - illuminating the entire field. Giovanni became quite excited and shook his head vigorously. Truly, those beautiful glowing triangular markers in the field, in their various colors of blue and orange, each trembled and flickered as if breathing.

30.translation: 'I've completely arrived at the heavenly field,' Giovanni said. 'And this train isn't burning any coal,' Giovanni said, extending his left hand and looking out the window ahead. 'Probably alcohol or electricity,' Campanella said. Goto-goto-goto-goto, that small, beautiful train, in the wind of celestial pampas grass, through the water of the Milky Way and the bluish faint glow of triangulation points, ran on and on without end. 'Ah, lupine flowers are blooming. Autumn has truly come,' Campanella said, pointing outside the window. In the short grass growing by the edge of the tracks, magnificent purple lupine flowers, as if carved from moonstone, were blooming. 'I want to jump down, pick some, and show you I can jump back on,'

31.translation: Giovanni's heart leaped as he said, 'That's it, I can't go on. It's gone too far back.' Before Campanella could say anything more, the next cluster of lupine flowers passed by, shining brightly. Then, one after another, countless cups of lupine flowers with yellow bottoms appeared before his eyes, welling up like rain, passing before him. The row of triangular markers smoked and burned, standing ever more brightly. 'Mother will forgive me, won't she?' Campanella suddenly asked, as if making up his mind, speaking quickly while hesitating slightly. Giovanni thought, (Ah, that's right, my mother is over there by that distant triangular marker that looks like a speck of dust, orange-colored, and she's thinking of me right now.)

32.translation: Thinking this, Giovanni was staring blankly and silent. 'I would do anything for my mother to be truly happy. But what on earth would be her greatest happiness?' Campenella seemed to be desperately holding back tears. 'But nothing terrible has happened to your mother, has it?' Giovanni exclaimed in surprise. 'I don't know. But I think when anyone does something truly good, that's their greatest happiness. So I think my mother will forgive me.' Campenella seemed to have made some real decision. Suddenly, inside the car, it became brilliantly white. Looking out, they saw that water was flowing silently and formlessly on a magnificent riverbed of the galaxy, as if collecting all diamonds, dew on grass, and all splendor. In the middle of this flow, they could see a vaguely pale blue island, illuminated by a halo.

33.translation: On that island's uneven ground stood a magnificent white cross that seemed to be cast from frozen Arctic clouds, topped with a bright golden halo, standing quietly and eternally. 'Hallelujah, Hallelujah.' Voices arose from both ahead and behind. When they looked back, the travelers in the car were all standing straight with their kimono folds hanging down, holding black Bibles to their chests, wearing crystal prayer beads, and each person humbly clasped their hands, praying in that direction. The two of them involuntarily stood up straight as well. Campagnola's cheeks were shining beautifully like the red of a ripe apple. And the island and the cross gradually moved backward. The opposite shore was palely glowing with smoke, and occasionally, what seemed to be silver grass swaying in the wind would suddenly flash its silver color like breath, and it seemed as though many lupine flowers were hiding and revealing themselves in the grass, appearing like gentle foxfire.

34.translation: For just a moment, the view between the river and the train was blocked by a row of susuki grass, and the Swan Island was visible a couple of times behind them, but soon it became far away and small, like a painting, and as the susuki rustled, it finally disappeared completely. Behind Giovanni, a tall Catholic nun with black clogs, who seemed to have boarded at some point, was staring straight down with her perfectly round green eyes, as if reverently listening for some word or sound coming from that direction. The travelers quietly returned to their seats, and the two of them quietly discussed, in different words, a new feeling that seemed like a fullness of sadness. 'We'll be at the Swan Station soon.' 'Yes, we'll arrive right at eleven o'clock.'

35.translation: Already, the green signal light and a faint white pillar flashed past the window, then a dim light like sulfur flames passed below the window as the train gradually slowed down. Soon, a row of beautiful, regularly spaced platform lights appeared, growing larger and spreading out. The two had arrived right in front of the large clock at Cygnus Station. On the cool autumn clock face, two blue-hot steel needles clearly pointed to eleven o'clock. Everyone got off at once, and the train cars became empty. 'Twenty-minute stop' was written below the clock. 'Shall we get off and take a look too?' Giovanni said. 'Let's get off.' The two of them sprang up at once, rushed out the door, and headed toward the ticket gate.

36.translation: However, at the ticket gate, only one bright purple-tinged electric lamp was lit, and there was no one there. Looking around, there was no sign of the station master or anyone who looked like a porter. The two of them came out into a small square, surrounded by ginkgo trees that looked like crystal carvings, in front of the station. From there, a wide path stretched straight into the blue light of the galaxy. The people who had gotten off before them were nowhere to be seen. As the two of them walked side by side along the white path, their shadows extended out in all directions, just like the shadows of two pillars in a room with windows on all sides, or like the spokes of two wheels extending out in all directions. And soon, they arrived at the beautiful riverbank that they had seen from that train. Campanella took a handful of the beautiful sand, spread it in his palm, and while rubbing it between his fingers, spoke as if in a dream.

37.translation: "This sand is all crystal. Inside, small fires are burning." 'That's right,' I thought, wondering where I had learned such a thing, while Giovanni also answered vaguely. The pebbles on the riverbank were all transparent, and indeed were crystal, topaz, or those that showed crumpled wrinkles, or also corundum that emitted a misty bluish-white light from its edges. Giovanni ran to that shore and dipped his hands in the water. But that strange galaxy water was even more transparent than hydrogen. Nevertheless, it was certainly flowing, as was evident from the fact that the parts of both wrists that were submerged looked slightly as if floating in mercury color, and the waves that struck against those wrists emitted a beautiful phosphorescence and seemed to flicker and burn. Looking upstream, below the cliff where silver grass grew abundantly, white rocks stretched out along the river as flat as a playground.

38.translation: There, small shadows of five or six people seemed to be digging something up or burying it, standing and crouching, with various tools occasionally flashing brightly. 'Let's go over there.' As if shouting in unison, the two of them ran in that direction. At the entrance of that white rock area stood a smooth ceramic sign that read 'Prioshin Coast,' and on the opposite shore, thin iron railings had been planted in places, and beautiful wooden benches were placed. 'Oh, look, there's something strange.' Campanella stopped, looking puzzled, and picked up something black, slender, and pointed from the rock that looked like a walnut. 'It's a walnut. Look, there are lots of them. They didn't wash up here. They're growing inside the rock.' 'This one is big, isn't it? Twice the size. And this one isn't damaged at all.'

39.translation: Let's hurry over there and see. They must be digging something." The two of them, holding jagged black nut-like fruits, went back toward where they had been before. On the left shore, the waves burned gently like soft lightning and came ashore, while on the right cliff, the ears of pampas grass, as if made of silver and shells, swayed. As they got closer, they saw a tall, scholarly-looking person with very thick glasses and wearing boots, who was frantically writing something in a notebook while giving various instructions to three assistants-like people who were either swinging a pickaxe or using a scope. "Don't break that protrusion there. Use the scope, use the scope. Oh, dig a bit further away. No, no, no. Why are you being so rough?" Looking, they saw that from the white, soft rock, a very large, bluish-gray animal's bone was lying crushed on its side, with more than half of it already dug out.

40.translation: And when I looked carefully, there were about ten square rocks with hoof prints, neatly cut out and numbered. 'Are you here for a visit?' asked the university professor-like person, flashing his glasses as he spoke to us. 'There were plenty of walnuts. Those would be from about 1.2 million years ago. Quite recent, really. This area was a coastline 1.2 million years ago, during the period after the Tertiary. Shells also come up from below. Where the river flows now, salt water used to ebb and flow. What animal is this? It's called a 'bosu,' oh, hey, put down that pickaxe. Carefully chip away with the chisel. It's called a 'bosu,' the ancestor of modern cattle, and there used to be many of them.' 'Are you making specimens?' 'No, we need it for proof.'

41.translation: From our perspective, this appears to be a thick, magnificent geological stratum, and there are various proofs that it was formed about 1.2 million years ago. But whether it looks like such a stratum to someone different from us, or whether it might appear as wind or empty space to them, that is the question. Do you understand? But, wait a minute. You can't go there with the scope. There should be ribs buried right underneath, shouldn't there?" The university professor hurriedly ran over. "It's time. Let's go." Campanella said while comparing the map with his wristwatch. "Ah, then we shall take our leave." Giovanni politely bowed to the university professor. "Is that so. Well, goodbye." The university professor began bustling around and supervising everywhere again. The two ran on top of the white rock, desperately trying not to be left behind by the train.

42.translation: And truly, they were able to run as if they were the wind. Their breath didn't run out, nor did their knees get hot. Giovanni thought that if they could run like this, they could run around the whole world. And so the two of them passed through that same riverbed from before, and the electric light at the ticket gate gradually grew larger, and soon the two were sitting in their original compartment seats, looking out the window at the direction they had just come from. 'May I get on here?' a raspy, yet kind, adult voice came from behind them. It was a man with a slightly tattered brown overcoat, wrapped in a white cloth, carrying his luggage split in two over his shoulders, with a red beard and a bent back. 'Yes, it's fine,' Giovanni replied, slightly shrugging his shoulders in greeting. The man, smiling faintly within his beard, slowly placed his luggage on the luggage rack.

43.translation: Giovanni felt somehow terribly lonely and sad, and while he was silently looking at the clock in front of him, something like a glass flute rang out from far ahead. The train was already moving quietly. Campanella was looking around at the ceiling of the car. A black beetle was perched on one of the lights, and its shadow was cast large on the ceiling. The red-bearded man was looking at Giovanni and Campanella with a somewhat nostalgic smile. The train was gradually getting faster, and pampas grass and rivers alternately shone outside the window. The red-bearded man asked the two of them somewhat hesitantly, 'Where are you gentlemen heading?' 'We're going as far as we can,' Giovanni answered somewhat awkwardly.

44.translation: That's nice. This train really goes anywhere, you know. Where are you going? Campenella suddenly asked as if in a quarrel, so Giovanni couldn't help but laugh. Then, the person sitting across the aisle, wearing a pointed hat and a large key at their waist, also glanced over and laughed, so Campenella also blushed and started laughing too. However, that person wasn't angry at all, and replied while their cheeks twitched. I'm getting off right there. I'm in the business of catching birds. What kind of birds? Cranes and wild geese. Also herons and swans. Are there many cranes? Of course there are! They've been crying out since a while ago. Didn't you hear? No. You can still hear them now, can't you?

45.translation: “Look, listen closely,” they said. The two raised their eyes and strained their ears. Between the clatter of the train and the wind rustling through the pampas grass, they heard a sound like water bubbling, *koron koron*. “Crane, what is it?” “Is it a crane? Or a heron?” “A heron.” Giovanni replied, thinking either would do. “That’s simple. Herons are formed from the solidified sand of the Milky Way, and they constantly return to the river. Wait by the riverbank. As soon as the heron comes down with its legs like this, before its feet even touch the ground, it’s pinned down. Then the heron freezes, feels at ease, and dies. The rest is obvious. They just become pressed leaves.”

vocabulary.translation: to lend an ear, to listen intently
to solidify, to condense (from 凝る - koru)

46.translation: ''Are you making herbarium sheets of egrets? Are they specimens?'' ''They're not specimens. Everyone eats them, don't they?'' ''That's strange.'' Campanella tilted his head. ''There's nothing strange or suspicious about it. Look.'' The man stood up, took a package from the shelf, and quickly unwrapped it. ''Now, look. I just brought them.'' ''They're really egrets.'' The two couldn't help but exclaim. The pure white egret bodies, shining like the Northern Cross they had just seen, were about ten of them, somewhat flattened, with their black legs drawn in, arranged like a relief. ''Their eyes are closed.'' Campanella gently touched the closed crescent-moon-shaped white eyes of the egret with his finger. The white spear-like feathers on its head were also properly attached. ''See, I told you so.''

47.translation: The bird catcher folded the wrapping cloth again, wrapped it round and round, and tied it with a string. 'Who on earth would eat a heron around here?' Giovanni wondered as he asked. 'Are herons delicious?' 'Yes, we get orders for them every day. But geese sell much better. Geese look much better, and first of all, they're easier to handle. See?' The bird catcher then unwrapped another package. There, in yellow, pale blue, and mottled colors, shining like some kind of light, were geese, lined up just like the heron a moment ago, with their beaks aligned and slightly flattened. 'These ones are ready to eat right away. How about trying a little?' The bird catcher lightly pulled on the leg of a yellow goose. Then, as if it were made of chocolate, it came off cleanly and beautifully.

48.translation: How about it? Have a little taste." The bird catcher tore it in two and handed it to Giovanni. Giovanni tried a little bite, thinking to himself, "What is this? So this is indeed some kind of sweet. It's more delicious than chocolate, but can birds really fly like this? This man must be some confectioner from the fields. But then again, it's quite pitiful that I'm eating this person's sweets while looking down on him." As he thought this, he continued to eat it heartily. "Have a little more." The bird catcher took out another package. Giovanni wanted to eat more, but when he said, "No, thank you," and declined, the bird catcher instead offered it to the person with the key in the opposite seat. "No, I couldn't accept something that's for sale.

vocabulary.translation: declined (past polite form of enryō suru)

49.translation: That person took off their hat. 'No, it's no trouble at all. How is business for the migratory birds this year?' 'Well, it's quite wonderful. The day before yesterday around the second period, why the lighthouse light was flashing irregularly, we got calls about malfunctions from both sides, but you see, it's not our doing - it's because the migratory birds are gathering all black and passing in front of the beacon light, so there's nothing we can do about it. Me, I'm terribly busy, even if they brought complaints to me, there's nothing I can do about it, so I told them, 'Take it to the general with the raspy cloak and the incredibly thin legs and mouth,' and I said that to them, haha.' Because the reeds had disappeared, light suddenly shot from the opposite field. 'Why are the herons causing so much trouble?' Campanella had been wanting to ask that since a while ago.

50.translation: "To eat a heron, you see," the bird catcher turned towards them. "You must hang it for ten days in the Milky Way's light, or else bury it in sand for three or four days. Then, all the mercury will evaporate, and it will become edible." "This isn't a bird. It's just candy, isn't it?" Campanella, who seemed to have been thinking the same thing, asked with a sudden resolve. The bird catcher, looking quite flustered, said, "That's right, we must get off here." As if saying this, he stood up, grabbed his luggage, and before they knew it, had disappeared. "Where could he have gone?" The two looked at each other, and the lighthouse keeper, grinning, stretched slightly and peeked out the window beside them. They looked in that direction too, and there was the bird catcher from just a moment ago, standing on a field of delicate, gleaming yellow and bluish phosphorescent grasses, his face serious, arms spread wide, gazing intently at the sky.

vocabulary.translation: light reflected on water; moonlight on water

51.translation: He must be heading over there. What a strange sight. He's definitely going to catch birds again. I hope the egrets come down before the train starts moving." As soon as he said this, from the empty light-purple sky, egrets just like the ones we saw earlier began to fall, crying 'gyaa gyaa' as they descended, as if snow were falling. Then the bird catcher, looking completely pleased as if this were exactly what he'd ordered, stood with his legs spread wide at a perfect sixty-degree angle. He caught the black legs of the descending egrets with his hands, one after another, and put them into a cloth bag. Then, inside the bag, the egrets glowed blue and flickered like fireflies for a while, but eventually they all turned dimly white and closed their eyes. However, more egrets managed to land safely on the sand of the Milky Way without being caught than those that were captured.

52.translation: That which he watched, as soon as his feet touched the sand, shrank and flattened out just like melting snow, and soon spread out over the sand and gravel like molten copper from a blast furnace. For a while, the shape of a bird remained imprinted on the sand, but after brightening and darkening two or three times, it eventually became completely the same color as its surroundings. The bird catcher, having caught about twenty birds, put them in a bag, then suddenly raised both hands in a posture like that of a soldier dying from a bullet. Just as he thought this, the bird catcher's form vanished, and instead, a familiar voice said beside Giovanni, 'Ah, what a relief. There's nothing better than earning just enough to suit your body.' Looking over, he saw that the bird catcher was neatly lining up the egrets he had caught and stacking them one by one.

53.translation: 「Why did you all come here at once from over there?」 Giovanni asked, feeling somehow as if it were obvious, yet not obvious, in a strange way. 「Why? Because I wanted to come. Where did you all come from?」 Giovanni tried to answer right away, but then, he just couldn't remember where he had come from at all. Campanella was also trying to remember something, his face bright red. 「Ah, from far away, I suppose.」 The bird catcher nodded as if he understood, without any fuss. 「This is already the end of the Cygnus District. Look over there. That's the famous Albireo Observatory.」 Outside the window, in what seemed like the middle of the Milky Way, filled as if with fireworks, four large black buildings stood, and on the flat roof of one of them, two large, transparent spheres of sapphire and topaz were quietly rotating in circles, their brilliance dazzling to the eye.

vocabulary.translation: at once, all at the same time

54.translation: The yellow gradually moved to the other side, while the small blue advanced toward this side. Soon, the two edges overlapped, forming a beautiful green double-convex lens shape. This gradually bulged in the middle until finally the blue one came completely to the front, creating a green center with a bright yellow ring. This then gradually moved sideways, repeating the previous lens shape in reverse, and finally separated completely. The sapphire went around to the other side, the yellow one advanced to this side, and it became exactly as it was before. Surrounded by the formless and silent water of the galaxy, the black observatory lay quietly as if sleeping. 'That's an instrument to measure the speed of the water. Water too...' just as the bird catcher was about to say, 'May I see your ticket.'

55.translation: Beside the three seats, a tall conductor with a red hat was standing straight and said something. The bird catcher silently reached into his pocket and took out a small piece of paper. The conductor glanced at it, quickly looked away, and as if to say (yours?), he gestured with his finger toward Giovanni and the others. 'Well,' Giovanni was troubled and fidgeted, then Campanella, as if it were nothing, took out a small mouse-colored ticket. Giovanni became completely flustered, wondering if perhaps it was in his jacket pocket, and when he put his hand in, he touched something large folded piece of paper. Thinking, 'Could there be such a thing in here?' he quickly pulled it out, and it was a green piece of paper about the size of a postcard folded in four.

56.translation: The conductor is holding out his hand, so I thought I shouldn't worry about it and just handed it over. The conductor straightened up properly and opened it carefully, looking at it. As he read it, he kept adjusting buttons on his jacket and things like that, and the lighthouse keeper below was also eagerly peering up at it. Giovanni thought that it must have been some kind of certificate, and he felt a warmth in his chest. 'Did you bring this from the three-dimensional world?' the conductor asked. 'I don't know what it is,' Giovanni said, looking up at it with a chuckle, feeling relieved that everything was alright. 'Very well. We will arrive at the Southern Cross around the third hour next,' the conductor said, handing the paper to Giovanni and walking away. Campanella seemed to have been eagerly waiting to find out what that piece of paper was, as he hurriedly peered in to see.

57.translation: Giovanni also really wanted to see it right away. However, it was something printed with about ten strange characters within a pattern like a black arabesque, and when he quietly gazed at it, he felt as if he were being drawn into it. Then the bird catcher glanced at it from the side and said in a surprised tone, 'Oh, this is quite remarkable. This is already a ticket that can take you even to the true heavens. Not just to heaven, but a free pass that allows you to go anywhere. If you possess this, then indeed, you should be able to travel anywhere on such an imperfect, illusory, fourth-dimensional Galactic Railroad. You two are quite something.' 'I don't understand,' Giovanni replied, blushing, as he folded it again and put it away. Feeling embarrassed, he and Campanella once again looked out the window, though he vaguely noticed that the bird catcher was occasionally glancing their way as if impressed by them.

58.translation: 'We'll be at Eagle Station soon,' Campanella said, comparing the three small pale blue triangular markers on the opposite bank with the map. For some reason, Giovanni suddenly felt overwhelming pity for the unknown bird catcher. He thought about how the catcher seemed pleased at catching a heron, happily wrapped it in white cloth, glanced at people's tickets with surprise, and hastily praised them. As he thought about these things one by one, Giovanni felt he wanted to give anything he had, anything he could eat, to that unknown bird catcher. He felt that if it would truly bring this man happiness, he would stand on the shining banks of the Milky Way for a hundred years, catching birds for him. He could no longer remain silent. He was about to ask, 'What is it that you truly want?' but then thought it would be too abrupt. As he hesitated and turned back, he saw that the bird catcher was no longer there.

59.translation: On the luggage rack, the white luggage was no longer visible. I thought perhaps he was outside the window, standing on tiptoes and preparing to catch herons as he looked up at the sky, so I quickly glanced in that direction, but outside there was only a beautiful expanse of sand and waves of white pampas grass. That wide back and pointed hat of the bird catcher were nowhere to be seen. 'Where could that person have gone?' Campanella muttered vaguely as well. 'Where could he have gone? Where on earth might we meet again? I really should have said something to that person.' 'Yes, I'm thinking the same thing.' 'I felt like that person was in the way, and so I'm terribly pained.' Giovanni thought that such strange feelings were happening to him for the first time, and that he had never said such things before. 'It smells like apples somehow. Did I just happen to be thinking about apples?'

60.translation: Campanella looked around wonderingly. 'It really smells like apples. And also of wild roses.' Giovanni looked around too, but it seemed that the smell was coming in from the window. Giovanni thought that wild roses shouldn't be blooming in autumn now. Then suddenly, there appeared a boy of about six with shiny black hair, wearing no red jacket buttons, looking terribly surprised, trembling all over, standing barefoot. Beside him stood a tall young man in neat black clothes, standing as if blown by the wind like a zelkova tree, firmly holding the boy's hand. 'Oh, where are we? Oh, how beautiful.' Behind the young man, a girl of about twelve with brown cute eyes, wearing a black cloak, was clinging to the young man's arm and looking out the window wonderingly.

61.translation: Ah, this is Lancashire. No, it's Connecticut. No, ah, we have come to the sky. We are going to heaven. Look, that sign is a sign from heaven. There is nothing to be afraid of anymore. We are being summoned by God." The young man in black said to the girl with joy. But for some reason, he also had deep wrinkles on his forehead, looked very tired, and forced a smile while making the boy sit next to Giovanni. Then he gently pointed to a seat next to Campanella for the girl. The girl obediently sat there and properly folded her hands together. "I think I'm going to my older sister's place," the boy who had just sat down said with a strange expression to the young man who had just sat down in the seat across from the lighthouse keeper.

62.translation: The young man couldn't say anything and looked sadly at the child's tangled, wet hair. The girl suddenly covered her face with both hands and burst into tears. 'Father and Kiku-nee are still busy with various work. But they'll be joining us soon. More importantly, Mother must have been waiting for so long. She's truly worried and waiting, wondering what my precious Tadashi is singing right now, whether he's playing with everyone, holding hands, running around the schoolyard in circles on a snowy morning. So let's hurry and go see Mother.' 'Un, but I wish I hadn't gotten on the boat.' 'Yes, but look, look up there, what do you think of that magnificent river? See, over there, during all of last summer, when we used to sing 'Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star' and rest, it was always vaguely visible white from the window, wasn't it?'

vocabulary.translation: honorific form of 'to be' or 'to come'

vocabulary.word: ツインクル、ツインクル、リトル、スター

63.translation: Over there. Look, isn't it beautiful, how it's shining so brightly." The sister, who had been crying, wiped her eyes with a handkerchief and looked outside. The young man said softly to the brother and sister as if teaching them, "We have nothing more to be sad about. We are traveling through such a wonderful place and will soon be going to where God is. There it is truly bright, fragrant, and filled with wonderful people. And those who were able to get on the boat in our place will surely all be rescued and go to their worried fathers and mothers and their own homes. Come on, it's almost time now, let's cheer up and sing happily as we go." The young man stroked the boy's wet-looking black hair, comforting everyone, and his own face gradually began to shine. "Where did you all come from?

64.translation: What happened?' the lighthouse keeper finally asked the young man as if he were beginning to understand. The young man smiled faintly. 'No, our ship hit an iceberg and sank. My father had returned to our home country two months earlier for urgent business, so we departed later. I was attending university and working as a private tutor. However, on the twelfth day, around today or yesterday, the ship hit an iceberg and began to sink all at once. There was some dim moonlight, but the fog was very thick. However, the boat on the port side was already half-damaged, so everyone couldn't possibly fit on it. The ship was about to sink any moment, and I desperately cried out, 'Please, let me take the small children on board!'

vocabulary.translation: port side (left side of a ship)

65.translation: The people nearby quickly made way and prayed for the children. However, between there and the boat, there were still small children and parents, and I didn't have the courage to push through. Still, I felt it was my duty to help these people, so I tried to push through the children in front. But then I thought that it would truly be these people's happiness if we all went to God together. After that, I thought that the sin of going against God was mine alone, and I definitely wanted to help them. But why couldn't I do it when I saw it? The children were being sent into the boat while their mother kissed them frantically like she was crazy, and their father stood straight, holding back his sadness, which was just heart-wrenching.

66.translation: Before long, the ship was rapidly sinking, so I had completely prepared myself, holding these two people, and just trying to stay afloat as we waited for the ship to sink. A life buoy came flying from who knows where, but it slipped away and went far off. I desperately let go of the deck grating and the three of us held on tightly to it. From somewhere, a voice shouted '[approximately two characters blank] number.' Immediately, everyone sang it in various languages at once. Just then, there was a loud noise, and as we thought we had fallen into the water and were being pulled by a vortex, I was holding these two people tightly, and then when I came to, we were already here. Their mother passed away the year before last. Yes, the boat must have been saved, after all, such skilled sailors were rowing it quickly away from the ship.

67.translation: From somewhere, the voice of a small fisherman could be heard, and Giovanni and Campanella both vaguely remembered various things they had forgotten until now, and their eyes grew hot. (Ah, wasn't that great ocean called the Pacific? In that sea at the northern edge where icebergs float, riding in a small boat, fighting against wind and freezing tidal waters and fierce cold, someone is working with all their might. I truly feel sorry for that person, and as if I can never make amends. What on earth should I do for that person's happiness?) Giovanni lowered his head and became completely dejected. 'I don't know what happiness is. Whatever suffering it may be, if it's something that happens while walking the right path, then both the uphill and downhill of the mountain pass are each steps that truly bring you closer to true happiness.'

vocabulary.translation: to become dejected, to be downcast
uphill and downhill of a mountain pass

68.translation: The lighthouse keeper was comforting him. 'Yes, that's right. All sorts of sorrows are all signs leading to the greatest happiness,' the young man answered as if praying. And that brother and sister were so exhausted that they had collapsed onto their seats and fallen asleep. On those bare feet from before, white soft shoes had somehow been put on. Goto-goto-goto-goto, the train advanced along the splendid shore of a phosphorescent river. Looking out the opposite window, the fields were just like a magic lantern projection. Hundreds and thousands of triangular markers of all different sizes could be seen, and on the larger ones, red dots were painted surveying flags were visible, and at the edge of the fields, they gathered together in great numbers like a faint bluish-white mist. From there or perhaps from further beyond, various shapes of indistinct smoke-like things were alternately being raised into the beautiful sky of balloon flower color.

69.translation: Indeed, that clear, beautiful wind was filled with the scent of roses. 'How is it? This is probably your first time seeing an apple like this.' The lighthouse keeper from across the aisle was holding a large apple, beautifully colored with gold and crimson, on his lap with both hands so it wouldn't fall. 'Oh, where did you come from? It's magnificent. Can apples like this grow around here?' The young man, seemingly truly surprised, stared at the pile of apples held in the lighthouse keeper's hands, narrowing his eyes and tilting his head, forgetting himself. 'No, please, take one. Please, do take one.' The young man took one and glanced toward Giovanni and the others. 'Come now, young gentlemen over there. How about it? Please take one.' Giovanni, being called 'young gentleman,' was a bit taken aback and remained silent, but Campanella

vocabulary.translation: forgetting oneself, losing track of oneself
to be taken aback, to be surprised

70.translation: "Thank you," Giovanni said. Then the young man took them himself and gave one to each of them, so Giovanni also stood up and said thank you. The lighthouse keeper finally had both arms free, so this time he himself gently placed one on the sleeping sister and brother's knees. "Thank you very much. Where can you get these? Such magnificent apples," the young man said, looking at them intently. "Of course, we do agriculture around here, but it's as if there's a promise that good things will grow on their own. Agriculture isn't that hard work either. As long as you plant the seeds you desire, they just grow and grow. Even the rice has no husk and is ten times larger, and has a wonderful fragrance. But in the place where you come from, there is no agriculture anymore. There aren't even any traces of apples or sweets, so they all become just a slight, different fragrance for each person and escape through the pores."

71.translation: The boy suddenly opened his eyes wide and said, 'Ah, I was just dreaming of my mother. Mother was in a wonderful place with nice shelves and books, looking at me and smiling and smiling and smiling. I said, 'Mother, shall I get you an apple?' and then I woke up. Ah, this is the same train car as before, isn't it?' 'The apple is right there. This uncle gave it to me,' the young man said. 'Thank you, Uncle. Oh, my sister is still sleeping. I'll wake her up. Sister, look, I got an apple. Wake up and see.' The sister laughed, rubbed her eyes as if dazzled, covered her eyes with both hands, and then looked at the apple. The boy was already eating it as if it were pie, and the beautifully peeled skin, before it could fall to the floor, curled up like a corkscrew and evaporated in a grayish light.

72.translation: The two of them carefully put the apples in their pockets. On the opposite bank downstream, a large forest of green trees could be seen, and on its branches were fully ripe, round, bright red fruits. In the middle of that forest stood a very tall triangular marker. From within the forest, along with the sounds of orchestral bells and xylophones, an indescribably beautiful sound color seemed to dissolve and soak in, flowing with the wind. The young man shuddered and trembled as if his body were shaking. When he quietly listened to that score, it seemed as if a bright field or carpet of yellow and light green spread out before him, and also as if pure white, wax-like dew were polishing the face of the sun. 'Oh, that crow,' the girl sitting next to Campanella, called Koppa, exclaimed. 'Those aren't crows. They're all nutcrackers,' Campanella said again, as if scolding casually. Giovanni couldn't help but laugh again, and the girl looked embarrassed.

vocabulary.translation: as if dissolving and soaking in

73.translation: Completely on the pale blue light of the riverbank, black birds were lined up densely and quietly receiving the faint glow of the river. 'Those are cormorants, you see, because the feathers on the back of their heads are stretched tight,' the young man said as if to reassure. The triangular marker in the blue forest ahead had now come completely in front of the train. At that moment, from the far rear of the train, the familiar tune of hymn number [about two characters blank] could be heard. It seemed to be sung by quite a large number of people in chorus. The young man's face suddenly turned pale, and he stood up as if to go there, but then thought better of it and sat down again. Kaoru covered her face with a handkerchief. Even Giovanni's nose somehow felt strange. But somehow, without anyone starting it, the song began and gradually became clearer and stronger.

74.translation: Giovanni and Campanella couldn't help but sing out together. And on the other side of the Milky Way, where the blue olive forest could not be seen, it gradually faded away behind them, and the strange sound of musical instruments flowing from there also became much fainter, drowned out by the sound of the train and the wind. 'There's a peacock,' Giovanni said. 'Oh, there were lots of them,' the girl replied. Giovanni saw, on top of the forest that had become tiny and now looked like another green shell, the light of peacocks quickly spreading, sometimes shining, as they spread and closed their wings. 'That's right, I could hear the sound of peacocks just now,' Campanella said to the boy. 'Yes, there were definitely about thirty of them. What sounded like harps were all peacocks,' the girl answered.

vocabulary.translation: to sing out, to burst into song
to wear away, to drown out

75.translation: Giovanni suddenly felt unable to speak and was overcome with sadness, almost as if he were about to say with a frightened face, 'Campanella, let's get off here and go play.' The river split into two. In the very center of that pitch-black island, a very tall tower had been built, and on top of it stood a man wearing loose-fitting clothes and a red hat. With red and blue flags in both hands, he was looking up at the sky and signaling. While Giovanni watched, the man kept waving the red flag, but suddenly lowered it as if to hide it behind him, and then raised the blue flag high, high up, shaking it violently like an orchestra conductor. Then, with a whoosh like the sound of rain, something pitch-black flew in clusters, like cannonballs, toward the other side of the river.

76.translation: Giovanni couldn't help but stick half his body out the window and look up that way. Under the beautiful, beautiful, spacious sky of the color of balloon flowers, truly tens of thousands of small birds, in groups after groups, were busily chirping and flying past. 'The birds are flying away,' Giovanni said outside the window. 'Look,' Campanella also looked at the sky. At that moment, the man in loose clothing on that scaffold suddenly raised a red flag and waved it frantically. Then the flocks of birds suddenly stopped passing by, and at the same time, a crushed-like sound of 'pshaan' occurred downstream, and then it was quiet for a while. Just then, that signalman in the red cap was again waving a blue flag and shouting. 'Now is the time to cross over, birds, now is the time to cross over.' That voice was also clearly heard. Together with that, again tens of thousands of flocks of birds flew straight across the sky.

vocabulary.translation: balloon flower (a type of purple flower)

77.translation: From the middle window where the two faces were visible, that girl stuck her face out, her beautiful cheeks shining as she looked up at the sky. 'Oh, look at all these birds, and isn't the sky beautiful!' The girl spoke to Giovanni, but Giovanni, thinking she was being conceited and annoying, remained silent with his mouth closed, looking up at the sky. The girl let out a small sigh and quietly returned to her seat. Campanella looked sympathetically back from the window and was looking at a map. 'Is he teaching those birds?' the girl quietly asked Campanella. 'He's signaling to the crossing birds. It must be because a signal rocket is going up from somewhere,' Campanella answered somewhat uncertainly. And then the carriage became quiet. Giovanni wanted to hide his face, but it was painful for him to show his face in the bright light, so he silently endured it, standing there and whistling.

vocabulary.translation: to look up at the sky

78.translation: Why am I so sad? I must have a cleaner, larger heart. On the far shore beyond, I can see a small blue flame like smoke. It is truly quiet and cold. I look at it to calm my heart. Giovanni held his hot, painful head with both hands and looked in that direction. (Ah, truly, is there no one who will go with me forever? Campanella is talking happily with that girl, and I feel truly sad.) Giovanni's eyes filled with tears again, and the Milky Way appeared blurry and white as if it had gone far away. At that moment, the train gradually moved away from the river and began to run along the top of a cliff. The opposite shore also had black cliffs that gradually became higher as they went downstream.

79.translation: And then I caught a glimpse of a large corn tree. Its leaves were curled in spirals, and beneath them, beautiful green husks with red hairs were already showing, and I could also catch a glimpse of pearl-like kernels. They were gradually increasing in number, and now they stood in rows between the cliff and the tracks. When Giovanni involuntarily pulled his face back from the window and looked out the other window, he saw that large corn trees had been planted almost all the way to the horizon of the beautiful sky prairie, swaying in the rustling wind. From the tips of their magnificent curled leaves, dewdrops were hanging as if they had absorbed all the sunlight during the day, sparkling and shining in red, green, and brilliant light. When Campanella said, 'Those are corn trees, aren't they?' to Giovanni, Giovanni's feelings just wouldn't calm down, so he just stared blankly at the prairie and said, 'I suppose so.'

80.translation: And so he answered. At that moment, the train gradually grew quiet as it passed several signals and the lights of signal boxes, and stopped at a small station. The bluish-white clock directly ahead clearly showed two o'clock, and its pendulum had stopped swaying in the still air. The train stood motionless as it precisely marked time with a regular 'tick-tock' in the quiet, quiet field. And then, from the far, far edge of the field, a faint, faint melody like a thread began to flow. 'It's Dvořák's New World Symphony,' my sister said softly, looking at me as if to herself. Truly, everyone in the car, even that tall young man in black, was all having gentle dreams. (In such a quiet, nice place, why can't I be happier? Why am I so lonely? But Campanella is too much, riding the train with me yet talking only with that girl.)

81.translation: I'm truly suffering.) Giovanni was again gazing out the opposite window, half-covering his face with his hands. A flute-like whistle, clear as glass, sounded, and the train began to move quietly. Kampanella also blew a lonely whistle as if circling the stars. 'Yes, yes, this is already a terrible high plateau.' From behind, someone who sounded like an elderly person was chatting briskly with someone, as if saying an old man had just woken up. 'Even corn needs holes drilled two shaku deep with sticks before it's sown there, otherwise it won't grow.' 'Is that so? It must be quite a distance to the river, then.' 'Yes, yes, the river is 2000 to 6000 feet away. It's become a terrible canyon.' That's right, wasn't this the Colorado Plateau? Giovanni couldn't help but think so. Kampanella was still blowing his lonely whistle alone, and the girl, with a face color like an apple wrapped in silk, was looking in Giovanni's direction.

82.translation: Suddenly the corn disappeared and a vast black field opened up completely. The New World Symphony became clearly visible rising from the horizon, and in the pitch-black field, an Indian with a white bird feather on his head, many stones on his arms and chest, and a small bow with an arrow strung was chasing after the train. 'Oh, look, it's an Indian. It's an Indian. Look.' The young man in black also woke up. Giovanni and Campanella also stood up. 'He's running, oh, he's running. He must be chasing us.' 'No, he's not chasing the train. He's either hunting or dancing.' The young man, as if forgetting where he was, said this while standing with his hands in his pockets. Truly, the Indian seemed to be dancing half the time.

83.translation: Even at first glance, it seemed that with better footing, more could be gained, and one could become more serious. Suddenly, those distinctly white feathers began to fall forward, and the Indian stopped dead in his tracks, quickly drawing his bow to the sky. From there, a crane wobbled and fell, landing in the large, outstretched arms of the Indian who had started running again. The Indian stood happily and laughed. Then, the shadow of him looking back with the crane grew smaller and smaller in the distance, the insulators on the telegraph poles glittered continuously, two of them lit up, and then it became a cornfield again. Looking out the window on this side, the train was truly running high on a steep cliff, and at the bottom of the valley, the river was still flowing wide and brightly. 'Yes, we're starting to descend from here. After all, this time we're going all the way down to that water surface at once, so it won't be easy.'

vocabulary.translation: to gain economically, to be profitable

84.translation: This is because of the slope, the train never comes from the other direction to here. Look, it's gradually getting faster, isn't it?" said the old man's voice. The train went down, down, down, down. When the railway crossed the edge of the cliff, the river below could be seen brightly. Giovanni gradually felt his mood brighten. When the train passed in front of a small hut and there was a child standing dejectedly, looking this way, he couldn't help but cry out in surprise. The train went on, on, on, on, running. The people in the car were half-leaning backward, clinging tightly to their seats. Giovanni couldn't help but laugh at Campanella. And the Milky Way had been flowing quite violently right beside the train until now, occasionally flashing as it flowed.

85.translation: Pale pink dianthus flowers were blooming here and there along the riverbank. The train was finally moving slowly as if it had settled down. On the opposite and this side of the bank, flags shaped like stars and pickaxes were standing. 'I wonder what that flag is,' Giovanni finally spoke. 'Well, I don't know. It's not on the map either. There's an iron boat there.' 'Ah.' 'Isn't this where they're building a bridge?' the girl said. 'Ah, that's an engineer's flag. They're doing bridge-building exercises. But I don't see any soldiers.' Just then, upstream on the opposite bank, the invisible water of the Milky Way suddenly flashed brightly, shot up high like a pillar with a loud crash, and made a violent sound. 'It's blasting, it's blasting!' Campanella exclaimed. The pillar-like water disappeared, and large salmon and trout were thrown into the air, shining white on their bellies, drawing circular patterns before falling back into the water.

86.translation: Giovanni said he felt so light he could jump up into the air. 'It's like an aerial engineering battalion. Look, even trout could be tossed up like this. I've never had such a pleasant journey. It's wonderful.' 'If you looked at that trout up close, it would be about this size. There are so many fish in this water.' 'Are there small fish too?' the girl asked, drawn into the conversation. 'There must be. If there are big ones, there must be small ones too. But they're far away, so I can't see the small ones right now.' Giovanni had completely cheered up and answered the girl with a laugh. 'Those must be the twin stars' palaces!' the boy suddenly shouted, pointing out the window. On the low hill to the right, two small palaces stood side by side, as if made of crystal.

87.dayTitle: Twin Stars and the River's Red Shore
87.translation: "What's the palace of the twin stars?" 'I've heard from Mother many times before. It's properly a small crystal palace with two of them lined up, so it must be that.' 'Let's go see.' 'I know too. The twin stars probably went out to play in the fields and fought with crows.' 'That's not it. You see, by the bank of the Milky Way, Mother told me...' 'And then the comet went 'Gee-Gee-Foo-Gee-Gee-Foo-Gee-Gee-Foo.' 'No, Grandma, that's not it. That's something else.' 'Then is he blowing a whistle over there?' 'He's gone to the sea now.' 'No, he shouldn't. He's already come back from the sea.' 'That's right. I know, I'll tell the story.' The opposite shore of the river suddenly turned red.

88.translation: The poplar trees and other things were silhouetted pitch black, and even the waves of the Milky Way, which couldn't be seen, occasionally glittered red like needles. Truly, on the opposite shore's field, a large crimson fire was burning, and its black smoke seemed to scorch the cool-looking sky of a deep gentian color. It burned redder than ruby, more transparent than lithium, and in a drunken state, that fire was burning. 'What kind of fire is that? What would need to be burned to create such a red, glowing fire?' Giovanni said. 'It's the fire of scorpions,' Campanella answered again, looking at the map. 'Oh, if it's about the fire of scorpions, I know about that,' said someone. 'What's the fire of scorpions?' Giovanni asked. 'It's when scorpions burn and die. That fire is still burning now, I've heard it many times from my father.' 'Scorpions... insects, right.'

89.translation: ''Yes, a scorpion is an insect. But it's a good insect.'' 'Scorpions aren't good insects. I've seen one preserved in alcohol at the museum. My teacher said it has a stinger on its tail and that if you're stung by it, you die.' 'That's right. But it's a good insect, my father said so. There was once a scorpion in the fields of old Valdra that killed and ate small insects to survive. Then one day, it was spotted by a weasel and was about to be eaten. The scorpion tried desperately to escape and escape, but in the end, it seemed like it would be caught by the weasel. Just then, there was suddenly a well in front of it, and it fell in. The scorpion couldn't climb out at all and began to drown. At that time, it is said that the scorpion prayed like this: 'Ah, I don't know how many lives I have taken until now, and now when my own life was about to be taken by the weasel, I tried so desperately to escape.'

90.translation: That's how it finally came to this. Ah, nothing can be relied upon. Why didn't I just give my body to the rabbits? Then they might have survived for one more day. Oh, God, please look at my heart. Please use my body for the sake of everyone's true happiness, without wasting this life in vain. That's what he said. And then, one day, he saw his own body become a beautiful, bright red fire that burned and illuminated the darkness of the night. My father said it's still burning now. That's truly that fire he was talking about. 'That's right. Look over there. Those triangular markers are arranged in the shape of a scorpion.' Giovanni saw, beyond that great fire, three triangular markers arranged like the arms of a scorpion, and five triangular markers arranged like the tail and stinger of a scorpion, all pointing toward him.

91.translation: And truly, that beautiful bright red scorpion's fire burned brightly without making a sound. As the fire gradually moved backward, everyone heard various bustling sounds of music, the scent of flowers and grass, whistling, and the murmuring voices of people. It felt as if there was soon a town or something nearby where a festival might be taking place. 'Make the Centaur dew fall,' the boy who had been sleeping next to Giovanni suddenly shouted, looking toward the opposite window. Ah, there stood a deep blue fir tree like a Christmas tree, and within it were many, many small electric lights attached, as if a thousand fireflies had gathered. 'Ah, that's right, tonight is the Centaur festival.' 'Ah, this is the Centaur village.'

92.translation: Kampanerra said immediately. 'If it's ball throwing, I definitely won't miss,' the boy said proudly. 'The Southern Cross will be here soon. Please prepare to get off,' the young man said to everyone. 'I'm also riding on the train a bit,' the boy said. The girl next to Kampanerra started to fidget and prepare to get off, but it seemed she didn't really want to part with Giovanni and the others. 'We have to get off here,' the young man said firmly, looking down at the boy. 'I don't want to. I want to ride on the train a bit more first,' Giovanni said, unable to hold back. 'Ride with us. We have tickets that can take us anywhere.' 'But we have to get off here now.'
